text:
The distribution of ions at the air/water interface is of considerable importance for many areas of atmospheric chemistry and environmental science. Yet, there is still much controversy as to the precise molecular arrangement at the interface. Indeed, the generally accepted picture is that polarizable ions accumulate at the air/water interface forming an EDL that influences the local alignment of interfacial water molecules. Here, we demonstrate that this well accepted model for the ion distribution at the air/water interface does not hold. In this work we present a systematic study on the ion distribution at the air/water interface for ten different electrolyte solutions by employing heterodyne-detected vibrational sum-frequency generation (HD-VSFG) and the use of neural network-assisted ab initio molecular dynamics simulations.

For sodium halides and similar salts our experiments reveal that the ions are not sitting at the very surface of solution but rather are primarily localized below the surface. Thus, there is an ion depleted surface layer with an ion enriched subsurface. Our results also indicate that the spectral changes caused by ions in solution result from ion–water interactions and not a macroscopic electric field such as predicted by the EDL. A stratification of the electrolyte interface for common salts is therefore established with only highly surface active ions, such as protons or perchlorates, following the traditional double-layer description. Our findings necessitate a reevaluation of many electrolyte-induced surface phenomena that rely on models of electrolyte surface interaction and ion distributions.
